{{smaller|{{nobold|(1871){{Clear}}{{lang|ja|名古屋藩}}}}}}
Owari Domain
{{smaller|{{nobold|(1610–1871){{Clear}}{{lang|ja|尾張藩}}}}}} |common_name = Owari Domain |subdivision = {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=Han}} |nation = |status_text = {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=Domain}} of Japan |government_type = Daimyō |title_leader = ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' |leader1 = {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshinao|en|Tokugawa Yoshinao|WD=Q1136186|lt=Tokugawa Yoshinao}} (first) |year_leader1 = 1607–1650 |leader2 = {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|en|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|WD=Q3530497|lt=Tokugawa Yoshikatsu}} (last) |year_leader2 = 1869–1871 |image =File:Nagoya Castle 7.jpg |image_caption = View of [[Nagoya Castle]] |capital = [[Nagoya Castle]] |coordinates = |political_subdiv = |today = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}} |year_start = 1600 |year_end = 1871 |event_start = |date_start = |event_end = |date_end = |event1 = |date_event1 = |event2 = |date_event2 = |event3 = |date_event3 = |event4 = |date_event4 = |event5 = |date_event5 = |life_span = |era = Edo period
Meiji period |event_pre = |date_pre = |event_post = |date_post = |image_flag = |image_border = |flag_type = |flag = |image_coat = [[File: Mitsubaaoi.svg |90px]] |symbol_type = ''[[Mon (emblem)|Mon]]'' of the [[Tokugawa clan]] |symbol = |image_map = File:180405 Tenshu and Honmaru Goten of Nagoya Castle.jpg |image_map_caption = Southeast corner of [[Nagoya Castle]] |stat_year1 = |stat_area1 = |stat_pop1 = |footnotes = }} [[File:Nagoya Castle 1979.1.48P01B.jpg|thumb|right|270px|Nagoya Castle was the administrative seat of the Owari Domain]] [[File:Ngy csl from kys csl.jpg|thumb|right|270px|In the modern era, there is a distinct feeling of separation that can be observed in Kiyosu. This is particularly evident when looking at the view of Nagoya Castle from the mock tower of {{ill|Kiyosu Castle|en|Kiyosu Castle|WD=Q1357421|lt=Kiyosu Castle}}, as captured in a photograph taken in February 2009]] [[File:Tokugawa Yoshikatsu.jpg|thumb|right|270px|{{ill|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|en|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|WD=Q3530497|lt=Tokugawa Yoshikatsu}}, final daimyo of Owari Domain]] The Owari-Han, also known as the '''Owari Domain''', was a significant feudal domain in [[Tokugawa shogunate|Japan]] during the Edo period.{{Cite book |last1=Wigen |first1=Kären |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H589DAAAQBAJ&dq=owari+domain&pg=PA58 |title=Cartographic Japan: A History in Maps |last2=Fumiko |first2=Sugimoto |last3=Karacas |first3=Cary |date=2016-03-16 |publisher=University of Chicago Press |isbn=978-0-226-07305-7 |language=en}} Situated in the western region of what is now {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}, it covered portions of {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari}}, {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino}}, and {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano}} provinces, with its central administration based at Nagoya Castle. At its zenith, the Owari Domain boasted an impressive rating of 619,500 koku, making it the largest landholding of the Tokugawa clan outside of the shogunal territories. The ruling clan of the Owari Domain was the Tokugawa clan, holding the prestigious position of the highest Rank among the gosanke. Additionally, the domain was sometimes referred to as the Nagoya Domain due to its association with Nagoya Castle.{{Cite book |last=Deal |first=William E. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=i0ni1NmbYe0C&dq=owari+domain&pg=PA82 |title=Handbook to Life in Medieval and Early Modern Japan |date=2007 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-533126-4 |language=en}} == History == Owari was initially ruled by {{ill|Fukushima Masanori|en|Fukushima Masanori|WD=Q1194995|lt=Fukushima Masanori}} with 240,000 koku until the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} in September 1600.{{Cite book |last=Walker |first=Brett L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QwK7BwAAQBAJ&dq=owari+domain&pg=PA104 |title=A Concise History of Japan |date=2015-02-26 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-1-316-23969-8 |language=en}} After his military achievements, he was reassigned to the {{ill|Hiroshima Domain|en|Hiroshima Domain|WD=Q1141203|lt=Hiroshima Domain}}. Matsudaira Tadayoshi, the fourth son of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, took control of the {{ill|Kiyosue Domain|en|Kiyosue Domain|WD=Q613073|lt=Kiyosue Domain}} with 520,000 koku after Fukushima's departure. Tadayoshi died in 1607 without an heir, leading the territory to become Tenryo.{{ill|Tokugawa Yoshinao|en|Tokugawa Yoshinao|WD=Q1136186|lt=Tokugawa Yoshinao}}, Ieyasu's ninth son, arrived from the Kai-Kofu Domain with 472,344 koku and established a clan in Owari, expanding the domain over time. By 1619, the domain had grown to 563,206 koku, with an additional 50,000 koku added in 1671 to assert its position as one of the top three families. The total koku reached 619,500, encompassing territories in {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino}}, {{ill|Mikawa Province|en|Mikawa Province|WD=Q768158|lt=Mikawa}}, {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano}}, {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Omi}}, and {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu}}. Despite officially having just under 620,000 koku, efforts to cultivate new fields led to an estimated actual koku of nearly 1 million, showcasing the domain's prosperity under Tokugawa Yoshinao's leadership. The first lord of the domain, Yoshinao Tokugawa, was still a child when he took office, so the early administration of the domain was carried out by Ieyasu's old retainers, but after he grew up, Yoshinao himself took charge of water supply, new rice field development, and annual taxes in order to increase rice production. He worked to establish the system and established the domain government. {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshinao|en|Tokugawa Yoshinao|WD=Q1136186|lt=Tokugawa Yoshinao}}, the initial ruler of the territory, took on the responsibilities of managing the water supply, developing new rice fields, and handling taxes to boost rice production and establish the domain's administrative structure. {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsutomo|en|Tokugawa Mitsutomo|WD=Q843245|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo}}, the second lord, focused on policies related to temples and shrines, but excessive reconstruction led to financial instability. Fiscal reforms were implemented, but natural disasters further weakened the domain's finances. [[Tokugawa Tsunamasa]], the third lord, had a strong connection to the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}} through his mother, contributing to the expansion and diversification of the [[Tokugawa clan]]'s influence and power. After the restoration of imperial rule, oshikatsu played a crucial role in urging [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] to resign. He aligned himself with the new government during the [[:en:Battle of Toba-Fushimi|Battle of Toba-Fushimi]] and actively suppressed the Sabaku faction during the {{ill|Aomatsuba Incident|en|Aomatsuba Incident|WD=Q4778660|lt=Aomatsuba Incident}}. Yoshikatsu Tokugawa, former feudal lord, was appointed leader of the Tokaido clans by the Meiji government after the Battle of Toba-Fushimi. He oversaw the {{ill|Tōkaidō (region)|en|Tōkaidō (region)|WD=Q1196306|lt=Tokaido}} fudai clans and sent envoys to neutralize Kokugaku groups to facilitate the passage of new government forces. In 1870, {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|en|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|WD=Q3530497|lt=Yoshikatsu}} absorbed the financially troubled {{ill|Takasu Domain|en|Takasu Domain|WD=Q3034539|lt=Takasu Domain}} to strengthen its stability. On July 14, 1871, the domain was abolished and transformed into [[:en:Nagoya Prefecture|Nagoya Prefecture]], which later merged with [[Inuyama Prefecture]] to become {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}. It further merged with {{ill|Nukata Prefecture|en|Nukata Prefecture|WD=Q3070843|lt=Nukata Prefecture}} to form present-day Aichi Prefecture. == List of daimyo == {| class=wikitable |+{{ill|Owari supported by the {{ill|Yanagawa Domain|en|Yanagawa Domain|WD=Q3034574|lt=Yanagawa Domain}} in {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province}} and the {{ill|Takasu Domain|en|Takasu Domain|WD=Q3034539|lt=Takasu Domain}} in Mino Province. ===Yanagawa Domain=== {{nihongo|Yanagawa Domain|柳河藩|Yanagawa-han|}} was a [[:en:Han (Japan)|feudal domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} Japan, in what is now eastern {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. It was centered around [[Yanagawa Castle]] in what is now the city of {{ill|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|en|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|WD=Q585630|lt=Yanagawa, Fukuoka}} and was ruled by the ''{{ill|Tozama daimyō|en|Tozama daimyō|WD=Q1195912|lt=Tozama daimyō}}'' {{ill|Tachibana clan (samurai)|en|Tachibana clan (samurai)|WD=Q2975377|lt=Tachibana clan}} for much of its history. The Yanagawa Domain provided 30,000 ''koku'' to the Owari Domain annually from 1683 to 1730, when Tokugawa Muneharu came to power and dissolved the domain. {| class=wikitable |+{{ill|Tachibana clan (samurai)|en|Tachibana clan (samurai)|WD=Q2975377|lt=Tachibana clan}}, 1620–1871 (''{{ill|Tozama daimyō|en|Tozama daimyō|WD=Q1195912|lt=Tozama daimyō}}'') ! #||Name || Tenure || Courtesy title || Domain|en|Takasu Domain|WD=Q3034539|lt=Takasu Domain}} (高須藩, Takasu-han) was a Japanese domain located in {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino Province}} (present-day {{ill|Kaizu|en|Kaizu|WD=Q844056|lt=Kaizu}}, {{ill|Gifu|en|Gifu|WD=Q45798|lt=Gifu}}). For most of its history, it was ruled by the Takasu-Matsudaira, a branch of the [[Tokugawa clan]] of Owari Domain. Matsudaira Katamori, Matsudaira Sadaaki, Tokugawa Yoshikatsu, and {{ill|Tokugawa Mochinaga|en|Tokugawa Mochinaga|WD=Q946037|lt=Tokugawa Mochinaga}}, four important figures in {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu}}-era Japan, were the sons of Matsudaira Yoshitatsu, one of Takasu's last daimyō. The Takasu Domain also provided 30,000 ''koku'' to the Owari Domain annually from 1700 to 1870, when it was merged with the Owari Domain. {| class=wikitable |+[[File: Japanese crest Owari mitu Aoi.svg|25px]]{{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira clan}}/[[Tokugawa clan]] (''{{ill|Shinpan (daimyo)|en|Shinpan (daimyo)|WD=Q1331112|lt=Shinpan}}'') 1700–1870 ! #||Name || Tenure || Courtesy title || Court Rank || ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' |- ||1||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshiyuki]]| 松平義行}}||1700–1715||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守) || Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||2||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitaka]]| 松平義孝}}||1715–1732||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下)||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||3||{{nihongo|[[:en:Matsudaira Yoshiatsu|Matsudaira Yoshiatsu]]| 松平義淳}}||1732–1739||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左近衛少将) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下)||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||4||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitoshi]]| 松平義敏}}||1739–1771||''Nakatsukasa no-daiyu'' (中務大輔)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||5||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitomo]]|松平義柄}}||1771–1777||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守); ''Jijū'' (侍従) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||6||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaita Yoshihiro]]| 松平 義裕}}||1777–1795||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守); ''Jijū'' (侍従) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||7||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshimasa]]| [松平義当}}||1795–1801||''Danjo-hitsu'' (弾正大弼) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||8||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshisue]]| 松平義居}}||1801–1804||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)；''Jijū'' (侍従)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||9||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshinari]]| 松平義和}}||1804–1832|||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||10||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitatsu]]| 松平義建}}||1832–1850||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||11||{{nihongo|[[Tokugawa Chikanaga]]| 徳川茂徳}}||1850–1858||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||12||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshimasa]]| 松平義端}}||1858–1860|| – none – || – none – ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||13||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitake]]| 松平義勇}}||1860–1869|| – none – ||Junior 5th Rank Lower Grade, (従五位)||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||14||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshinari]]| 松平義生}}||1869–1870||- none – || – none – ||30,000 ''koku'' |} ==See also== *{{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=Han system}} *[[:en:List of Han|List of Han]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Domains of Chūbu}} [[en:Owari Domain]] [[de:Owari (Han)]] [[fa:قلمرو اواری]] [[fr:Domaine d'Owari]] [[ja:尾張藩]] [[ko:오와리번]] [[ru:Овари (княжество)]] [[th:แคว้นศักดินาโอวาริ]] [[zh:尾張藩]] {{wikidata link|Q1062072}} [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

{{smaller|{{nobold|(1871){{Clear}}{{lang|ja|名古屋藩}}}}}}
Owari Domain
{{smaller|{{nobold|(1610–1871){{Clear}}{{lang|ja|尾張藩}}}}}} |common_name = Owari Domain |subdivision = {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=Han}} |nation = |status_text = {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=Domain}} of Japan |government_type = Daimyō |title_leader = ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' |leader1 = {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshinao|en|Tokugawa Yoshinao|WD=Q1136186|lt=Tokugawa Yoshinao}} (first) |year_leader1 = 1607–1650 |leader2 = {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|en|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|WD=Q3530497|lt=Tokugawa Yoshikatsu}} (last) |year_leader2 = 1869–1871 |image =File:Nagoya Castle 7.jpg |image_caption = View of [[Nagoya Castle]] |capital = [[Nagoya Castle]] |coordinates = |political_subdiv = |today = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}} |year_start = 1600 |year_end = 1871 |event_start = |date_start = |event_end = |date_end = |event1 = |date_event1 = |event2 = |date_event2 = |event3 = |date_event3 = |event4 = |date_event4 = |event5 = |date_event5 = |life_span = |era = Edo period
Meiji period |event_pre = |date_pre = |event_post = |date_post = |image_flag = |image_border = |flag_type = |flag = |image_coat = [[File: Mitsubaaoi.svg |90px]] |symbol_type = ''[[Mon (emblem)|Mon]]'' of the [[Tokugawa clan]] |symbol = |image_map = File:180405 Tenshu and Honmaru Goten of Nagoya Castle.jpg |image_map_caption = Southeast corner of [[Nagoya Castle]] |stat_year1 = |stat_area1 = |stat_pop1 = |footnotes = }} [[File:Nagoya Castle 1979.1.48P01B.jpg|thumb|right|270px|Nagoya Castle was the administrative seat of the Owari Domain]] [[File:Ngy csl from kys csl.jpg|thumb|right|270px|In the modern era, there is a distinct feeling of separation that can be observed in Kiyosu. This is particularly evident when looking at the view of Nagoya Castle from the mock tower of {{ill|Kiyosu Castle|en|Kiyosu Castle|WD=Q1357421|lt=Kiyosu Castle}}, as captured in a photograph taken in February 2009]] [[File:Tokugawa Yoshikatsu.jpg|thumb|right|270px|{{ill|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|en|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|WD=Q3530497|lt=Tokugawa Yoshikatsu}}, final daimyo of Owari Domain]] The Owari-Han, also known as the '''Owari Domain''', was a significant feudal domain in [[Tokugawa shogunate|Japan]] during the Edo period.{{Cite book |last1=Wigen |first1=Kären |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H589DAAAQBAJ&dq=owari+domain&pg=PA58 |title=Cartographic Japan: A History in Maps |last2=Fumiko |first2=Sugimoto |last3=Karacas |first3=Cary |date=2016-03-16 |publisher=University of Chicago Press |isbn=978-0-226-07305-7 |language=en}} Situated in the western region of what is now {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}, it covered portions of {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari}}, {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino}}, and {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano}} provinces, with its central administration based at Nagoya Castle. At its zenith, the Owari Domain boasted an impressive rating of 619,500 koku, making it the largest landholding of the Tokugawa clan outside of the shogunal territories. The ruling clan of the Owari Domain was the Tokugawa clan, holding the prestigious position of the highest Rank among the gosanke. Additionally, the domain was sometimes referred to as the Nagoya Domain due to its association with Nagoya Castle.{{Cite book |last=Deal |first=William E. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=i0ni1NmbYe0C&dq=owari+domain&pg=PA82 |title=Handbook to Life in Medieval and Early Modern Japan |date=2007 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-533126-4 |language=en}} == History == Owari was initially ruled by {{ill|Fukushima Masanori|en|Fukushima Masanori|WD=Q1194995|lt=Fukushima Masanori}} with 240,000 koku until the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} in September 1600.{{Cite book |last=Walker |first=Brett L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QwK7BwAAQBAJ&dq=owari+domain&pg=PA104 |title=A Concise History of Japan |date=2015-02-26 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-1-316-23969-8 |language=en}} After his military achievements, he was reassigned to the {{ill|Hiroshima Domain|en|Hiroshima Domain|WD=Q1141203|lt=Hiroshima Domain}}. Matsudaira Tadayoshi, the fourth son of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, took control of the {{ill|Kiyosue Domain|en|Kiyosue Domain|WD=Q613073|lt=Kiyosue Domain}} with 520,000 koku after Fukushima's departure. Tadayoshi died in 1607 without an heir, leading the territory to become Tenryo.{{ill|Tokugawa Yoshinao|en|Tokugawa Yoshinao|WD=Q1136186|lt=Tokugawa Yoshinao}}, Ieyasu's ninth son, arrived from the Kai-Kofu Domain with 472,344 koku and established a clan in Owari, expanding the domain over time. By 1619, the domain had grown to 563,206 koku, with an additional 50,000 koku added in 1671 to assert its position as one of the top three families. The total koku reached 619,500, encompassing territories in {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino}}, {{ill|Mikawa Province|en|Mikawa Province|WD=Q768158|lt=Mikawa}}, {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano}}, {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Omi}}, and {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu}}. Despite officially having just under 620,000 koku, efforts to cultivate new fields led to an estimated actual koku of nearly 1 million, showcasing the domain's prosperity under Tokugawa Yoshinao's leadership. The first lord of the domain, Yoshinao Tokugawa, was still a child when he took office, so the early administration of the domain was carried out by Ieyasu's old retainers, but after he grew up, Yoshinao himself took charge of water supply, new rice field development, and annual taxes in order to increase rice production. He worked to establish the system and established the domain government. {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshinao|en|Tokugawa Yoshinao|WD=Q1136186|lt=Tokugawa Yoshinao}}, the initial ruler of the territory, took on the responsibilities of managing the water supply, developing new rice fields, and handling taxes to boost rice production and establish the domain's administrative structure. {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsutomo|en|Tokugawa Mitsutomo|WD=Q843245|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo}}, the second lord, focused on policies related to temples and shrines, but excessive reconstruction led to financial instability. Fiscal reforms were implemented, but natural disasters further weakened the domain's finances. [[Tokugawa Tsunamasa]], the third lord, had a strong connection to the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}} through his mother, contributing to the expansion and diversification of the [[Tokugawa clan]]'s influence and power. After the restoration of imperial rule, oshikatsu played a crucial role in urging [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] to resign. He aligned himself with the new government during the [[:en:Battle of Toba-Fushimi|Battle of Toba-Fushimi]] and actively suppressed the Sabaku faction during the {{ill|Aomatsuba Incident|en|Aomatsuba Incident|WD=Q4778660|lt=Aomatsuba Incident}}. Yoshikatsu Tokugawa, former feudal lord, was appointed leader of the Tokaido clans by the Meiji government after the Battle of Toba-Fushimi. He oversaw the {{ill|Tōkaidō (region)|en|Tōkaidō (region)|WD=Q1196306|lt=Tokaido}} fudai clans and sent envoys to neutralize Kokugaku groups to facilitate the passage of new government forces. In 1870, {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|en|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|WD=Q3530497|lt=Yoshikatsu}} absorbed the financially troubled {{ill|Takasu Domain|en|Takasu Domain|WD=Q3034539|lt=Takasu Domain}} to strengthen its stability. On July 14, 1871, the domain was abolished and transformed into [[:en:Nagoya Prefecture|Nagoya Prefecture]], which later merged with [[Inuyama Prefecture]] to become {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}. It further merged with {{ill|Nukata Prefecture|en|Nukata Prefecture|WD=Q3070843|lt=Nukata Prefecture}} to form present-day Aichi Prefecture. == List of daimyo == {| class=wikitable |+{{ill|Owari Tokugawa family|en|Owari Tokugawa family|WD=Q841740|lt=Owari Tokugawa family}}, 1600–1871 (''[[:en:Fudai daimyo|Fudai daimyo]]'') ! #||Name || Tenure || Courtesy title || Court Rank || supported by the {{ill|Yanagawa Domain|en|Yanagawa Domain|WD=Q3034574|lt=Yanagawa Domain}} in {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province}} and the {{ill|Takasu Domain|en|Takasu Domain|WD=Q3034539|lt=Takasu Domain}} in Mino Province. ===Yanagawa Domain=== {{nihongo|Yanagawa Domain|柳河藩|Yanagawa-han|}} was a [[:en:Han (Japan)|feudal domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} Japan, in what is now eastern {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. It was centered around [[Yanagawa Castle]] in what is now the city of {{ill|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|en|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|WD=Q585630|lt=Yanagawa, Fukuoka}} and was ruled by the ''{{ill|Tozama daimyō|en|Tozama daimyō|WD=Q1195912|lt=Tozama daimyō}}'' {{ill|Tachibana clan (samurai)|en|Tachibana clan (samurai)|WD=Q2975377|lt=Tachibana clan}} for much of its history. The Yanagawa Domain provided 30,000 ''koku'' to the Owari Domain annually from 1683 to 1730, when Tokugawa Muneharu came to power and dissolved the domain. {| class=wikitable |+{{ill|Tachibana clan (samurai)|en|Tachibana clan (samurai)|WD=Q2975377|lt=Tachibana clan}}, 1620–1871 (''{{ill|Tozama daimyō|en|Tozama daimyō|WD=Q1195912|lt=Tozama daimyō}}'') ! #||Name || Tenure || Courtesy title || (立花鑑広)||1830–1833|| -None- ||Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) ||109,000 ''koku'' |- ||11||Tachibana Akinobu (立花鑑備)||1833–1845||''Sakon'e-no-shōgen'' (左近将監) ||Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||109,000 ''koku'' |- ||12||Tachibana Akitomo (立花鑑寛)||1845–1871||''Sakon'e-no-shōgen'' (左近将監) ||Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||109,000 ->114,000 ''koku'' |} ===Takasu Domain=== The {{ill|Takasu Domain|en|Takasu Domain|WD=Q3034539|lt=Takasu Domain}} (高須藩, Takasu-han) was a Japanese domain located in {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino Province}} (present-day {{ill|Kaizu|en|Kaizu|WD=Q844056|lt=Kaizu}}, {{ill|Gifu|en|Gifu|WD=Q45798|lt=Gifu}}). For most of its history, it was ruled by the Takasu-Matsudaira, a branch of the [[Tokugawa clan]] of Owari Domain. Matsudaira Katamori, Matsudaira Sadaaki, Tokugawa Yoshikatsu, and {{ill|Tokugawa Mochinaga|en|Tokugawa Mochinaga|WD=Q946037|lt=Tokugawa Mochinaga}}, four important figures in {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu}}-era Japan, were the sons of Matsudaira Yoshitatsu, one of Takasu's last daimyō. The Takasu Domain also provided 30,000 ''koku'' to the Owari Domain annually from 1700 to 1870, when it was merged with the Owari Domain. {| class=wikitable |+[[File: Japanese crest Owari mitu Aoi.svg|25px]]{{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira clan}}/[[Tokugawa clan]] (''{{ill|Shinpan (daimyo)|en|Shinpan (daimyo)|WD=Q1331112|lt=Shinpan}}'') 1700–1870 ! #||Name || Tenure || Courtesy title || Court Rank || ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' |- ||1||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshiyuki]]| 松平義行}}||1700–1715||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守) || Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||2||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitaka]]| 松平義孝}}||1715–1732||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下)||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||3||{{nihongo|[[:en:Matsudaira Yoshiatsu|Matsudaira Yoshiatsu]]| 松平義淳}}||1732–1739||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左近衛少将) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下)||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||4||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitoshi]]| 松平義敏}}||1739–1771||''Nakatsukasa no-daiyu'' (中務大輔)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||5||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitomo]]|松平義柄}}||1771–1777||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守); ''Jijū'' (侍従) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||6||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaita Yoshihiro]]| 松平 義裕}}||1777–1795||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守); ''Jijū'' (侍従) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||7||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshimasa]]| [松平義当}}||1795–1801||''Danjo-hitsu'' (弾正大弼) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||8||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshisue]]| 松平義居}}||1801–1804||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)；''Jijū'' (侍従)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||9||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshinari]]| 松平義和}}||1804–1832|||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||10||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitatsu]]| 松平義建}}||1832–1850||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||11||{{nihongo|[[Tokugawa Chikanaga]]| 徳川茂徳}}||1850–1858||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||12||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshimasa]]| 松平義端}}||1858–1860|| – none – || – none – ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||13||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitake]]| 松平義勇}}||1860–1869|| – none – ||Junior 5th Rank Lower Grade, (従五位)||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||14||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshinari]]| 松平義生}}||1869–1870||- none – || – none – ||30,000 ''koku'' |} ==See also== *{{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=Han system}} *[[:en:List of Han|List of Han]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Domains of Chūbu}} [[en:Owari Domain]] [[de:Owari (Han)]] [[fa:قلمرو اواری]] [[fr:Domaine d'Owari]] [[ja:尾張藩]] [[ko:오와리번]] [[ru:Овари (княжество)]] [[th:แคว้นศักดินาโอวาริ]] [[zh:尾張藩]] {{wikidata link|Q1062072}} [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

{{smaller|{{nobold|(1871){{Clear}}{{lang|ja|名古屋藩}}}}}}
Owari Domain
{{smaller|{{nobold|(1610–1871){{Clear}}{{lang|ja|尾張藩}}}}}} |common_name = Owari Domain |subdivision = {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=Han}} |nation = |status_text = {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=Domain}} of Japan |government_type = Daimyō |title_leader = ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' |leader1 = {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshinao|en|Tokugawa Yoshinao|WD=Q1136186|lt=Tokugawa Yoshinao}} (first) |year_leader1 = 1607–1650 |leader2 = {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|en|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|WD=Q3530497|lt=Tokugawa Yoshikatsu}} (last) |year_leader2 = 1869–1871 |image =File:Nagoya Castle 7.jpg |image_caption = View of [[Nagoya Castle]] |capital = [[Nagoya Castle]] |coordinates = |political_subdiv = |today = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}} |year_start = 1600 |year_end = 1871 |event_start = |date_start = |event_end = |date_end = |event1 = |date_event1 = |event2 = |date_event2 = |event3 = |date_event3 = |event4 = |date_event4 = |event5 = |date_event5 = |life_span = |era = Edo period
Meiji period |event_pre = |date_pre = |event_post = |date_post = |image_flag = |image_border = |flag_type = |flag = |image_coat = [[File: Mitsubaaoi.svg |90px]] |symbol_type = ''[[Mon (emblem)|Mon]]'' of the [[Tokugawa clan]] |symbol = |image_map = File:180405 Tenshu and Honmaru Goten of Nagoya Castle.jpg |image_map_caption = Southeast corner of [[Nagoya Castle]] |stat_year1 = |stat_area1 = |stat_pop1 = |footnotes = }} [[File:Nagoya Castle 1979.1.48P01B.jpg|thumb|right|270px|Nagoya Castle was the administrative seat of the Owari Domain]] [[File:Ngy csl from kys csl.jpg|thumb|right|270px|In the modern era, there is a distinct feeling of separation that can be observed in Kiyosu. This is particularly evident when looking at the view of Nagoya Castle from the mock tower of {{ill|Kiyosu Castle|en|Kiyosu Castle|WD=Q1357421|lt=Kiyosu Castle}}, as captured in a photograph taken in February 2009]] [[File:Tokugawa Yoshikatsu.jpg|thumb|right|270px|{{ill|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|en|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|WD=Q3530497|lt=Tokugawa Yoshikatsu}}, final daimyo of Owari Domain]] The Owari-Han, also known as the '''Owari Domain''', was a significant feudal domain in [[Tokugawa shogunate|Japan]] during the Edo period.{{Cite book |last1=Wigen |first1=Kären |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H589DAAAQBAJ&dq=owari+domain&pg=PA58 |title=Cartographic Japan: A History in Maps |last2=Fumiko |first2=Sugimoto |last3=Karacas |first3=Cary |date=2016-03-16 |publisher=University of Chicago Press |isbn=978-0-226-07305-7 |language=en}} Situated in the western region of what is now {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}, it covered portions of {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari}}, {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino}}, and {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano}} provinces, with its central administration based at Nagoya Castle. At its zenith, the Owari Domain boasted an impressive rating of 619,500 koku, making it the largest landholding of the Tokugawa clan outside of the shogunal territories. The ruling clan of the Owari Domain was the Tokugawa clan, holding the prestigious position of the highest Rank among the gosanke. Additionally, the domain was sometimes referred to as the Nagoya Domain due to its association with Nagoya Castle.{{Cite book |last=Deal |first=William E. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=i0ni1NmbYe0C&dq=owari+domain&pg=PA82 |title=Handbook to Life in Medieval and Early Modern Japan |date=2007 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-533126-4 |language=en}} == History == Owari was initially ruled by {{ill|Fukushima Masanori|en|Fukushima Masanori|WD=Q1194995|lt=Fukushima Masanori}} with 240,000 koku until the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} in September 1600.{{Cite book |last=Walker |first=Brett L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QwK7BwAAQBAJ&dq=owari+domain&pg=PA104 |title=A Concise History of Japan |date=2015-02-26 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-1-316-23969-8 |language=en}} After his military achievements, he was reassigned to the {{ill|Hiroshima Domain|en|Hiroshima Domain|WD=Q1141203|lt=Hiroshima Domain}}. Matsudaira Tadayoshi, the fourth son of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, took control of the {{ill|Kiyosue Domain|en|Kiyosue Domain|WD=Q613073|lt=Kiyosue Domain}} with 520,000 koku after Fukushima's departure. Tadayoshi died in 1607 without an heir, leading the territory to become Tenryo.{{ill|Tokugawa Yoshinao|en|Tokugawa Yoshinao|WD=Q1136186|lt=Tokugawa Yoshinao}}, Ieyasu's ninth son, arrived from the Kai-Kofu Domain with 472,344 koku and established a clan in Owari, expanding the domain over time. By 1619, the domain had grown to 563,206 koku, with an additional 50,000 koku added in 1671 to assert its position as one of the top three families. The total koku reached 619,500, encompassing territories in {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino}}, {{ill|Mikawa Province|en|Mikawa Province|WD=Q768158|lt=Mikawa}}, {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano}}, {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Omi}}, and {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu}}. Despite officially having just under 620,000 koku, efforts to cultivate new fields led to an estimated actual koku of nearly 1 million, showcasing the domain's prosperity under Tokugawa Yoshinao's leadership. The first lord of the domain, Yoshinao Tokugawa, was still a child when he took office, so the early administration of the domain was carried out by Ieyasu's old retainers, but after he grew up, Yoshinao himself took charge of water supply, new rice field development, and annual taxes in order to increase rice production. He worked to establish the system and established the domain government. {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshinao|en|Tokugawa Yoshinao|WD=Q1136186|lt=Tokugawa Yoshinao}}, the initial ruler of the territory, took on the responsibilities of managing the water supply, developing new rice fields, and handling taxes to boost rice production and establish the domain's administrative structure. {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsutomo|en|Tokugawa Mitsutomo|WD=Q843245|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo}}, the second lord, focused on policies related to temples and shrines, but excessive reconstruction led to financial instability. Fiscal reforms were implemented, but natural disasters further weakened the domain's finances. [[Tokugawa Tsunamasa]], the third lord, had a strong connection to the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}} through his mother, contributing to the expansion and diversification of the [[Tokugawa clan]]'s influence and power. After the restoration of imperial rule, oshikatsu played a crucial role in urging [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] to resign. He aligned himself with the new government during the [[:en:Battle of Toba-Fushimi|Battle of Toba-Fushimi]] and actively suppressed the Sabaku faction during the {{ill|Aomatsuba Incident|en|Aomatsuba Incident|WD=Q4778660|lt=Aomatsuba Incident}}. Yoshikatsu Tokugawa, former feudal lord, was appointed leader of the Tokaido clans by the Meiji government after the Battle of Toba-Fushimi. He oversaw the {{ill|Tōkaidō (region)|en|Tōkaidō (region)|WD=Q1196306|lt=Tokaido}} fudai clans and sent envoys to neutralize Kokugaku groups to facilitate the passage of new government forces. In 1870, {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|en|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|WD=Q3530497|lt=Yoshikatsu}} absorbed the financially troubled {{ill|Takasu Domain|en|Takasu Domain|WD=Q3034539|lt=Takasu Domain}} to strengthen its stability. On July 14, 1871, the domain was abolished and transformed into [[:en:Nagoya Prefecture|Nagoya Prefecture]], which later merged with [[Inuyama Prefecture]] to become {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}. It further merged with {{ill|Nukata Prefecture|en|Nukata Prefecture|WD=Q3070843|lt=Nukata Prefecture}} to form present-day Aichi Prefecture. == List of daimyo == {| class=wikitable |+{{ill|Owari Tokugawa family|en|Owari Tokugawa family|WD=Q841740|lt=Owari Tokugawa family}}, 1600–1871 (''[[:en:Fudai daimyo|Fudai daimyo]]'') ! #||Name || Tenure || Courtesy title || Court Rank || Yoshikatsu|WD=Q3530497|lt=Tokugawa Yoshikatsu}} (徳川慶恕)||1869–1871|| ''Chunagon Jusanmi'' (中納言従三位)||Junior 4th Rank Lower Grade (従四位下)|| 619,500 ''koku'' |- |} ==Sub-domains== The Owari Domain was supported by the {{ill|Yanagawa Domain|en|Yanagawa Domain|WD=Q3034574|lt=Yanagawa Domain}} in {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province}} and the {{ill|Takasu Domain|en|Takasu Domain|WD=Q3034539|lt=Takasu Domain}} in Mino Province. ===Yanagawa Domain=== {{nihongo|Yanagawa Domain|柳河藩|Yanagawa-han|}} was a [[:en:Han (Japan)|feudal domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} Japan, in what is now eastern {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. It was centered around [[Yanagawa Castle]] in what is now the city of {{ill|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|en|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|WD=Q585630|lt=Yanagawa, Fukuoka}} and was ruled by the ''{{ill|Tozama daimyō|en|Tozama daimyō|WD=Q1195912|lt=Tozama daimyō}}'' {{ill|Tachibana clan (samurai)|en|Tachibana clan (samurai)|WD=Q2975377|lt=Tachibana clan}} for much of its history. The Yanagawa Domain provided 30,000 ''koku'' to the Owari Domain annually from 1683 to 1730, when Tokugawa Muneharu came to power and dissolved the domain. {| class=wikitable |+{{ill|Tachibana clan (samurai)|en|Tachibana clan (samurai)|WD=Q2975377|lt=Tachibana clan}}, 1620–1871 (''{{ill|Tozama daimyō|en|Tozama daimyō|WD=Q1195912|lt=Tozama daimyō}}'') ! #||Name || Tenure || Courtesy title || ''koku'' |- ||6||Tachibana Sadanori (立花貞則)||1744–1746||''Hida-no-kami''(飛騨守) ||Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) ||109,000 ''koku'' |- ||7||Tachibana Akinao (立花鑑広)||1830–1833|| -None- ||Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) ||109,000 ''koku'' |- ||11||Tachibana Akinobu (立花鑑備)||1833–1845||''Sakon'e-no-shōgen'' (左近将監) ||Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||109,000 ''koku'' |- ||12||Tachibana Akitomo (立花鑑寛)||1845–1871||''Sakon'e-no-shōgen'' (左近将監) ||Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||109,000 ->114,000 ''koku'' |} ===Takasu Domain=== The {{ill|Takasu Domain|en|Takasu Domain|WD=Q3034539|lt=Takasu Domain}} (高須藩, Takasu-han) was a Japanese domain located in {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino Province}} (present-day {{ill|Kaizu|en|Kaizu|WD=Q844056|lt=Kaizu}}, {{ill|Gifu|en|Gifu|WD=Q45798|lt=Gifu}}). For most of its history, it was ruled by the Takasu-Matsudaira, a branch of the [[Tokugawa clan]] of Owari Domain. Matsudaira Katamori, Matsudaira Sadaaki, Tokugawa Yoshikatsu, and {{ill|Tokugawa Mochinaga|en|Tokugawa Mochinaga|WD=Q946037|lt=Tokugawa Mochinaga}}, four important figures in {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu}}-era Japan, were the sons of Matsudaira Yoshitatsu, one of Takasu's last daimyō. The Takasu Domain also provided 30,000 ''koku'' to the Owari Domain annually from 1700 to 1870, when it was merged with the Owari Domain. {| class=wikitable |+[[File: Japanese crest Owari mitu Aoi.svg|25px]]{{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira clan}}/[[Tokugawa clan]] (''{{ill|Shinpan (daimyo)|en|Shinpan (daimyo)|WD=Q1331112|lt=Shinpan}}'') 1700–1870 ! #||Name || Tenure || Courtesy title || Court Rank || ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' |- ||1||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshiyuki]]| 松平義行}}||1700–1715||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守) || Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||2||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitaka]]| 松平義孝}}||1715–1732||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下)||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||3||{{nihongo|[[:en:Matsudaira Yoshiatsu|Matsudaira Yoshiatsu]]| 松平義淳}}||1732–1739||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左近衛少将) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下)||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||4||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitoshi]]| 松平義敏}}||1739–1771||''Nakatsukasa no-daiyu'' (中務大輔)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||5||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitomo]]|松平義柄}}||1771–1777||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守); ''Jijū'' (侍従) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||6||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaita Yoshihiro]]| 松平 義裕}}||1777–1795||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守); ''Jijū'' (侍従) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||7||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshimasa]]| [松平義当}}||1795–1801||''Danjo-hitsu'' (弾正大弼) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||8||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshisue]]| 松平義居}}||1801–1804||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)；''Jijū'' (侍従)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||9||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshinari]]| 松平義和}}||1804–1832|||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||10||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitatsu]]| 松平義建}}||1832–1850||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||11||{{nihongo|[[Tokugawa Chikanaga]]| 徳川茂徳}}||1850–1858||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||12||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshimasa]]| 松平義端}}||1858–1860|| – none – || – none – ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||13||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitake]]| 松平義勇}}||1860–1869|| – none – ||Junior 5th Rank Lower Grade, (従五位)||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||14||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshinari]]| 松平義生}}||1869–1870||- none – || – none – ||30,000 ''koku'' |} ==See also== *{{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=Han system}} *[[:en:List of Han|List of Han]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Domains of Chūbu}} [[en:Owari Domain]] [[de:Owari (Han)]] [[fa:قلمرو اواری]] [[fr:Domaine d'Owari]] [[ja:尾張藩]] [[ko:오와리번]] [[ru:Овари (княжество)]] [[th:แคว้นศักดินาโอวาริ]] [[zh:尾張藩]] {{wikidata link|Q1062072}} [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

{{smaller|{{nobold|(1871){{Clear}}{{lang|ja|名古屋藩}}}}}}
Owari Domain
{{smaller|{{nobold|(1610–1871){{Clear}}{{lang|ja|尾張藩}}}}}} |common_name = Owari Domain |subdivision = {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=Han}} |nation = |status_text = {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=Domain}} of Japan |government_type = Daimyō |title_leader = ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' |leader1 = {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshinao|en|Tokugawa Yoshinao|WD=Q1136186|lt=Tokugawa Yoshinao}} (first) |year_leader1 = 1607–1650 |leader2 = {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|en|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|WD=Q3530497|lt=Tokugawa Yoshikatsu}} (last) |year_leader2 = 1869–1871 |image =File:Nagoya Castle 7.jpg |image_caption = View of [[Nagoya Castle]] |capital = [[Nagoya Castle]] |coordinates = |political_subdiv = |today = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}} |year_start = 1600 |year_end = 1871 |event_start = |date_start = |event_end = |date_end = |event1 = |date_event1 = |event2 = |date_event2 = |event3 = |date_event3 = |event4 = |date_event4 = |event5 = |date_event5 = |life_span = |era = Edo period
Meiji period |event_pre = |date_pre = |event_post = |date_post = |image_flag = |image_border = |flag_type = |flag = |image_coat = [[File: Mitsubaaoi.svg |90px]] |symbol_type = ''[[Mon (emblem)|Mon]]'' of the [[Tokugawa clan]] |symbol = |image_map = File:180405 Tenshu and Honmaru Goten of Nagoya Castle.jpg |image_map_caption = Southeast corner of [[Nagoya Castle]] |stat_year1 = |stat_area1 = |stat_pop1 = |footnotes = }} [[File:Nagoya Castle 1979.1.48P01B.jpg|thumb|right|270px|Nagoya Castle was the administrative seat of the Owari Domain]] [[File:Ngy csl from kys csl.jpg|thumb|right|270px|In the modern era, there is a distinct feeling of separation that can be observed in Kiyosu. This is particularly evident when looking at the view of Nagoya Castle from the mock tower of {{ill|Kiyosu Castle|en|Kiyosu Castle|WD=Q1357421|lt=Kiyosu Castle}}, as captured in a photograph taken in February 2009]] [[File:Tokugawa Yoshikatsu.jpg|thumb|right|270px|{{ill|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|en|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|WD=Q3530497|lt=Tokugawa Yoshikatsu}}, final daimyo of Owari Domain]] The Owari-Han, also known as the '''Owari Domain''', was a significant feudal domain in [[Tokugawa shogunate|Japan]] during the Edo period.{{Cite book |last1=Wigen |first1=Kären |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H589DAAAQBAJ&dq=owari+domain&pg=PA58 |title=Cartographic Japan: A History in Maps |last2=Fumiko |first2=Sugimoto |last3=Karacas |first3=Cary |date=2016-03-16 |publisher=University of Chicago Press |isbn=978-0-226-07305-7 |language=en}} Situated in the western region of what is now {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}, it covered portions of {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari}}, {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino}}, and {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano}} provinces, with its central administration based at Nagoya Castle. At its zenith, the Owari Domain boasted an impressive rating of 619,500 koku, making it the largest landholding of the Tokugawa clan outside of the shogunal territories. The ruling clan of the Owari Domain was the Tokugawa clan, holding the prestigious position of the highest Rank among the gosanke. Additionally, the domain was sometimes referred to as the Nagoya Domain due to its association with Nagoya Castle.{{Cite book |last=Deal |first=William E. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=i0ni1NmbYe0C&dq=owari+domain&pg=PA82 |title=Handbook to Life in Medieval and Early Modern Japan |date=2007 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-533126-4 |language=en}} == History == Owari was initially ruled by {{ill|Fukushima Masanori|en|Fukushima Masanori|WD=Q1194995|lt=Fukushima Masanori}} with 240,000 koku until the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} in September 1600.{{Cite book |last=Walker |first=Brett L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QwK7BwAAQBAJ&dq=owari+domain&pg=PA104 |title=A Concise History of Japan |date=2015-02-26 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-1-316-23969-8 |language=en}} After his military achievements, he was reassigned to the {{ill|Hiroshima Domain|en|Hiroshima Domain|WD=Q1141203|lt=Hiroshima Domain}}. Matsudaira Tadayoshi, the fourth son of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, took control of the {{ill|Kiyosue Domain|en|Kiyosue Domain|WD=Q613073|lt=Kiyosue Domain}} with 520,000 koku after Fukushima's departure. Tadayoshi died in 1607 without an heir, leading the territory to become Tenryo.{{ill|Tokugawa Yoshinao|en|Tokugawa Yoshinao|WD=Q1136186|lt=Tokugawa Yoshinao}}, Ieyasu's ninth son, arrived from the Kai-Kofu Domain with 472,344 koku and established a clan in Owari, expanding the domain over time. By 1619, the domain had grown to 563,206 koku, with an additional 50,000 koku added in 1671 to assert its position as one of the top three families. The total koku reached 619,500, encompassing territories in {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino}}, {{ill|Mikawa Province|en|Mikawa Province|WD=Q768158|lt=Mikawa}}, {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano}}, {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Omi}}, and {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu}}. Despite officially having just under 620,000 koku, efforts to cultivate new fields led to an estimated actual koku of nearly 1 million, showcasing the domain's prosperity under Tokugawa Yoshinao's leadership. The first lord of the domain, Yoshinao Tokugawa, was still a child when he took office, so the early administration of the domain was carried out by Ieyasu's old retainers, but after he grew up, Yoshinao himself took charge of water supply, new rice field development, and annual taxes in order to increase rice production. He worked to establish the system and established the domain government. {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshinao|en|Tokugawa Yoshinao|WD=Q1136186|lt=Tokugawa Yoshinao}}, the initial ruler of the territory, took on the responsibilities of managing the water supply, developing new rice fields, and handling taxes to boost rice production and establish the domain's administrative structure. {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsutomo|en|Tokugawa Mitsutomo|WD=Q843245|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo}}, the second lord, focused on policies related to temples and shrines, but excessive reconstruction led to financial instability. Fiscal reforms were implemented, but natural disasters further weakened the domain's finances. [[Tokugawa Tsunamasa]], the third lord, had a strong connection to the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}} through his mother, contributing to the expansion and diversification of the [[Tokugawa clan]]'s influence and power. After the restoration of imperial rule, oshikatsu played a crucial role in urging [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] to resign. He aligned himself with the new government during the [[:en:Battle of Toba-Fushimi|Battle of Toba-Fushimi]] and actively suppressed the Sabaku faction during the {{ill|Aomatsuba Incident|en|Aomatsuba Incident|WD=Q4778660|lt=Aomatsuba Incident}}. Yoshikatsu Tokugawa, former feudal lord, was appointed leader of the Tokaido clans by the Meiji government after the Battle of Toba-Fushimi. He oversaw the {{ill|Tōkaidō (region)|en|Tōkaidō (region)|WD=Q1196306|lt=Tokaido}} fudai clans and sent envoys to neutralize Kokugaku groups to facilitate the passage of new government forces. In 1870, {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|en|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|WD=Q3530497|lt=Yoshikatsu}} absorbed the financially troubled {{ill|Takasu Domain|en|Takasu Domain|WD=Q3034539|lt=Takasu Domain}} to strengthen its stability. On July 14, 1871, the domain was abolished and transformed into [[:en:Nagoya Prefecture|Nagoya Prefecture]], which later merged with [[Inuyama Prefecture]] to become {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}. It further merged with {{ill|Nukata Prefecture|en|Nukata Prefecture|WD=Q3070843|lt=Nukata Prefecture}} to form present-day Aichi Prefecture. == List of daimyo == {| class=wikitable |+{{ill|Owari Tokugawa family|en|Owari Tokugawa family|WD=Q841740|lt=Owari Tokugawa family}}, 1600–1871 (''[[:en:Fudai daimyo|Fudai daimyo]]'') ! #||Name || Tenure || Courtesy title || Court Rank || Yoshinori|WD=Q3530498|lt=Tokugawa Yoshinori}} (徳川義宜)||1863–1869|| ''Konoe, Kon no Chujo'' (近衛、今中将)|| Junior 4th Rank Lower Grade (従四位下)|| 619,500 ''koku'' |- |17||{{ill|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|en|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|WD=Q3530497|lt=Tokugawa Yoshikatsu}} (徳川慶恕)||1869–1871|| ''Chunagon Jusanmi'' (中納言従三位)||Junior 4th Rank Lower Grade (従四位下)|| 619,500 ''koku'' |- |} ==Sub-domains== The Owari Domain was supported by the {{ill|Yanagawa Domain|en|Yanagawa Domain|WD=Q3034574|lt=Yanagawa Domain}} in {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province}} and the {{ill|Takasu Domain|en|Takasu Domain|WD=Q3034539|lt=Takasu Domain}} in Mino Province. ===Yanagawa Domain=== {{nihongo|Yanagawa Domain|柳河藩|Yanagawa-han|}} was a [[:en:Han (Japan)|feudal domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} Japan, in what is now eastern {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. It was centered around [[Yanagawa Castle]] in what is now the city of {{ill|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|en|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|WD=Q585630|lt=Yanagawa, Fukuoka}} and was ruled by the ''{{ill|Tozama daimyō|en|Tozama daimyō|WD=Q1195912|lt=Tozama daimyō}}'' {{ill|Tachibana clan (samurai)|en|Tachibana clan (samurai)|WD=Q2975377|lt=Tachibana clan}} for much of its history. The Yanagawa Domain provided 30,000 ''koku'' to the Owari Domain annually from 1683 to 1730, when Tokugawa Muneharu came to power and dissolved the domain. {| class=wikitable |+{{ill|Tachibana clan (samurai)|en|Tachibana clan (samurai)|WD=Q2975377|lt=Tachibana clan}}, 1620–1871 (''{{ill|Tozama daimyō|en|Tozama daimyō|WD=Q1195912|lt=Tozama daimyō}}'') ! #||Name || Tenure || Courtesy title || Court Rank || ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' |- ||1||{{ill|Tachibana Muneshige|en|Tachibana Muneshige|WD=Q1071770|lt=Tachibana Muneshige}} (立花宗茂)||1620–1638|| ''Sakon'e-no-shōgen'' (左近将監) ||Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||109,000 ''koku'' |- ||2||Tachibana Tadashige (立花忠重)||1638–1664|| ''Sakon'e-no-shōgen'' (左近将監) ||Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||109,000 ''koku'' |- ||12||Tachibana Akitomo (立花鑑寛)||1845–1871||''Sakon'e-no-shōgen'' (左近将監) ||Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||109,000 ->114,000 ''koku'' |} ===Takasu Domain=== The {{ill|Takasu Domain|en|Takasu Domain|WD=Q3034539|lt=Takasu Domain}} (高須藩, Takasu-han) was a Japanese domain located in {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino Province}} (present-day {{ill|Kaizu|en|Kaizu|WD=Q844056|lt=Kaizu}}, {{ill|Gifu|en|Gifu|WD=Q45798|lt=Gifu}}). For most of its history, it was ruled by the Takasu-Matsudaira, a branch of the [[Tokugawa clan]] of Owari Domain. Matsudaira Katamori, Matsudaira Sadaaki, Tokugawa Yoshikatsu, and {{ill|Tokugawa Mochinaga|en|Tokugawa Mochinaga|WD=Q946037|lt=Tokugawa Mochinaga}}, four important figures in {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu}}-era Japan, were the sons of Matsudaira Yoshitatsu, one of Takasu's last daimyō. The Takasu Domain also provided 30,000 ''koku'' to the Owari Domain annually from 1700 to 1870, when it was merged with the Owari Domain. {| class=wikitable |+[[File: Japanese crest Owari mitu Aoi.svg|25px]]{{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira clan}}/[[Tokugawa clan]] (''{{ill|Shinpan (daimyo)|en|Shinpan (daimyo)|WD=Q1331112|lt=Shinpan}}'') 1700–1870 ! #||Name || Tenure || Courtesy title || Court Rank || ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' |- ||1||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshiyuki]]| 松平義行}}||1700–1715||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守) || Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||2||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitaka]]| 松平義孝}}||1715–1732||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下)||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||3||{{nihongo|[[:en:Matsudaira Yoshiatsu|Matsudaira Yoshiatsu]]| 松平義淳}}||1732–1739||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左近衛少将) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下)||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||4||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitoshi]]| 松平義敏}}||1739–1771||''Nakatsukasa no-daiyu'' (中務大輔)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||5||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitomo]]|松平義柄}}||1771–1777||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守); ''Jijū'' (侍従) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||6||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaita Yoshihiro]]| 松平 義裕}}||1777–1795||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守); ''Jijū'' (侍従) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||7||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshimasa]]| [松平義当}}||1795–1801||''Danjo-hitsu'' (弾正大弼) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||8||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshisue]]| 松平義居}}||1801–1804||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)；''Jijū'' (侍従)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||9||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshinari]]| 松平義和}}||1804–1832|||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||10||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitatsu]]| 松平義建}}||1832–1850||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||11||{{nihongo|[[Tokugawa Chikanaga]]| 徳川茂徳}}||1850–1858||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||12||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshimasa]]| 松平義端}}||1858–1860|| – none – || – none – ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||13||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitake]]| 松平義勇}}||1860–1869|| – none – ||Junior 5th Rank Lower Grade, (従五位)||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||14||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshinari]]| 松平義生}}||1869–1870||- none – || – none – ||30,000 ''koku'' |} ==See also== *{{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=Han system}} *[[:en:List of Han|List of Han]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Domains of Chūbu}} [[en:Owari Domain]] [[de:Owari (Han)]] [[fa:قلمرو اواری]] [[fr:Domaine d'Owari]] [[ja:尾張藩]] [[ko:오와리번]] [[ru:Овари (княжество)]] [[th:แคว้นศักดินาโอวาริ]] [[zh:尾張藩]] {{wikidata link|Q1062072}} [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

{{smaller|{{nobold|(1871){{Clear}}{{lang|ja|名古屋藩}}}}}}
Owari Domain
{{smaller|{{nobold|(1610–1871){{Clear}}{{lang|ja|尾張藩}}}}}} |common_name = Owari Domain |subdivision = {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=Han}} |nation = |status_text = {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=Domain}} of Japan |government_type = Daimyō |title_leader = ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' |leader1 = {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshinao|en|Tokugawa Yoshinao|WD=Q1136186|lt=Tokugawa Yoshinao}} (first) |year_leader1 = 1607–1650 |leader2 = {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|en|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|WD=Q3530497|lt=Tokugawa Yoshikatsu}} (last) |year_leader2 = 1869–1871 |image =File:Nagoya Castle 7.jpg |image_caption = View of [[Nagoya Castle]] |capital = [[Nagoya Castle]] |coordinates = |political_subdiv = |today = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}} |year_start = 1600 |year_end = 1871 |event_start = |date_start = |event_end = |date_end = |event1 = |date_event1 = |event2 = |date_event2 = |event3 = |date_event3 = |event4 = |date_event4 = |event5 = |date_event5 = |life_span = |era = Edo period
Meiji period |event_pre = |date_pre = |event_post = |date_post = |image_flag = |image_border = |flag_type = |flag = |image_coat = [[File: Mitsubaaoi.svg |90px]] |symbol_type = ''[[Mon (emblem)|Mon]]'' of the [[Tokugawa clan]] |symbol = |image_map = File:180405 Tenshu and Honmaru Goten of Nagoya Castle.jpg |image_map_caption = Southeast corner of [[Nagoya Castle]] |stat_year1 = |stat_area1 = |stat_pop1 = |footnotes = }} [[File:Nagoya Castle 1979.1.48P01B.jpg|thumb|right|270px|Nagoya Castle was the administrative seat of the Owari Domain]] [[File:Ngy csl from kys csl.jpg|thumb|right|270px|In the modern era, there is a distinct feeling of separation that can be observed in Kiyosu. This is particularly evident when looking at the view of Nagoya Castle from the mock tower of {{ill|Kiyosu Castle|en|Kiyosu Castle|WD=Q1357421|lt=Kiyosu Castle}}, as captured in a photograph taken in February 2009]] [[File:Tokugawa Yoshikatsu.jpg|thumb|right|270px|{{ill|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|en|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|WD=Q3530497|lt=Tokugawa Yoshikatsu}}, final daimyo of Owari Domain]] The Owari-Han, also known as the '''Owari Domain''', was a significant feudal domain in [[Tokugawa shogunate|Japan]] during the Edo period.{{Cite book |last1=Wigen |first1=Kären |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H589DAAAQBAJ&dq=owari+domain&pg=PA58 |title=Cartographic Japan: A History in Maps |last2=Fumiko |first2=Sugimoto |last3=Karacas |first3=Cary |date=2016-03-16 |publisher=University of Chicago Press |isbn=978-0-226-07305-7 |language=en}} Situated in the western region of what is now {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}, it covered portions of {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari}}, {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino}}, and {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano}} provinces, with its central administration based at Nagoya Castle. At its zenith, the Owari Domain boasted an impressive rating of 619,500 koku, making it the largest landholding of the Tokugawa clan outside of the shogunal territories. The ruling clan of the Owari Domain was the Tokugawa clan, holding the prestigious position of the highest Rank among the gosanke. Additionally, the domain was sometimes referred to as the Nagoya Domain due to its association with Nagoya Castle.{{Cite book |last=Deal |first=William E. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=i0ni1NmbYe0C&dq=owari+domain&pg=PA82 |title=Handbook to Life in Medieval and Early Modern Japan |date=2007 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-533126-4 |language=en}} == History == Owari was initially ruled by {{ill|Fukushima Masanori|en|Fukushima Masanori|WD=Q1194995|lt=Fukushima Masanori}} with 240,000 koku until the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} in September 1600.{{Cite book |last=Walker |first=Brett L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QwK7BwAAQBAJ&dq=owari+domain&pg=PA104 |title=A Concise History of Japan |date=2015-02-26 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-1-316-23969-8 |language=en}} After his military achievements, he was reassigned to the {{ill|Hiroshima Domain|en|Hiroshima Domain|WD=Q1141203|lt=Hiroshima Domain}}. Matsudaira Tadayoshi, the fourth son of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, took control of the {{ill|Kiyosue Domain|en|Kiyosue Domain|WD=Q613073|lt=Kiyosue Domain}} with 520,000 koku after Fukushima's departure. Tadayoshi died in 1607 without an heir, leading the territory to become Tenryo.{{ill|Tokugawa Yoshinao|en|Tokugawa Yoshinao|WD=Q1136186|lt=Tokugawa Yoshinao}}, Ieyasu's ninth son, arrived from the Kai-Kofu Domain with 472,344 koku and established a clan in Owari, expanding the domain over time. By 1619, the domain had grown to 563,206 koku, with an additional 50,000 koku added in 1671 to assert its position as one of the top three families. The total koku reached 619,500, encompassing territories in {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino}}, {{ill|Mikawa Province|en|Mikawa Province|WD=Q768158|lt=Mikawa}}, {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano}}, {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Omi}}, and {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu}}. Despite officially having just under 620,000 koku, efforts to cultivate new fields led to an estimated actual koku of nearly 1 million, showcasing the domain's prosperity under Tokugawa Yoshinao's leadership. The first lord of the domain, Yoshinao Tokugawa, was still a child when he took office, so the early administration of the domain was carried out by Ieyasu's old retainers, but after he grew up, Yoshinao himself took charge of water supply, new rice field development, and annual taxes in order to increase rice production. He worked to establish the system and established the domain government. {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshinao|en|Tokugawa Yoshinao|WD=Q1136186|lt=Tokugawa Yoshinao}}, the initial ruler of the territory, took on the responsibilities of managing the water supply, developing new rice fields, and handling taxes to boost rice production and establish the domain's administrative structure. {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsutomo|en|Tokugawa Mitsutomo|WD=Q843245|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo}}, the second lord, focused on policies related to temples and shrines, but excessive reconstruction led to financial instability. Fiscal reforms were implemented, but natural disasters further weakened the domain's finances. [[Tokugawa Tsunamasa]], the third lord, had a strong connection to the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}} through his mother, contributing to the expansion and diversification of the [[Tokugawa clan]]'s influence and power. After the restoration of imperial rule, oshikatsu played a crucial role in urging [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] to resign. He aligned himself with the new government during the [[:en:Battle of Toba-Fushimi|Battle of Toba-Fushimi]] and actively suppressed the Sabaku faction during the {{ill|Aomatsuba Incident|en|Aomatsuba Incident|WD=Q4778660|lt=Aomatsuba Incident}}. Yoshikatsu Tokugawa, former feudal lord, was appointed leader of the Tokaido clans by the Meiji government after the Battle of Toba-Fushimi. He oversaw the {{ill|Tōkaidō (region)|en|Tōkaidō (region)|WD=Q1196306|lt=Tokaido}} fudai clans and sent envoys to neutralize Kokugaku groups to facilitate the passage of new government forces. In 1870, {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|en|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|WD=Q3530497|lt=Yoshikatsu}} absorbed the financially troubled {{ill|Takasu Domain|en|Takasu Domain|WD=Q3034539|lt=Takasu Domain}} to strengthen its stability. On July 14, 1871, the domain was abolished and transformed into [[:en:Nagoya Prefecture|Nagoya Prefecture]], which later merged with [[Inuyama Prefecture]] to become {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}. It further merged with {{ill|Nukata Prefecture|en|Nukata Prefecture|WD=Q3070843|lt=Nukata Prefecture}} to form present-day Aichi Prefecture. == List of daimyo == {| class=wikitable |+{{ill|Owari Tokugawa family|en|Owari Tokugawa family|WD=Q841740|lt=Owari Tokugawa family}}, 1600–1871 (''[[:en:Fudai daimyo|Fudai daimyo]]'') ! #||Name || Tenure || Courtesy title || Court Rank || Mochinaga|WD=Q946037|lt=Tokugawa Mochinaga}} (徳川茂徳)||1858–1863|| ''Gon Dainagon'' (権大納言)||Junior 4th Rank Lower Grade (従四位下)|| 619,500 ''koku'' |- |16||{{ill|Tokugawa Yoshinori|en|Tokugawa Yoshinori|WD=Q3530498|lt=Tokugawa Yoshinori}} (徳川義宜)||1863–1869|| ''Konoe, Kon no Chujo'' (近衛、今中将)|| Junior 4th Rank Lower Grade (従四位下)|| 619,500 ''koku'' |- |17||{{ill|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|en|Tokugawa Yoshikatsu|WD=Q3530497|lt=Tokugawa Yoshikatsu}} (徳川慶恕)||1869–1871|| ''Chunagon Jusanmi'' (中納言従三位)||Junior 4th Rank Lower Grade (従四位下)|| 619,500 ''koku'' |- |} ==Sub-domains== The Owari Domain was supported by the {{ill|Yanagawa Domain|en|Yanagawa Domain|WD=Q3034574|lt=Yanagawa Domain}} in {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province}} and the {{ill|Takasu Domain|en|Takasu Domain|WD=Q3034539|lt=Takasu Domain}} in Mino Province. ===Yanagawa Domain=== {{nihongo|Yanagawa Domain|柳河藩|Yanagawa-han|}} was a [[:en:Han (Japan)|feudal domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} Japan, in what is now eastern {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. It was centered around [[Yanagawa Castle]] in what is now the city of {{ill|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|en|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|WD=Q585630|lt=Yanagawa, Fukuoka}} and was ruled by the ''{{ill|Tozama daimyō|en|Tozama daimyō|WD=Q1195912|lt=Tozama daimyō}}'' {{ill|Tachibana clan (samurai)|en|Tachibana clan (samurai)|WD=Q2975377|lt=Tachibana clan}} for much of its history. The Yanagawa Domain provided 30,000 ''koku'' to the Owari Domain annually from 1683 to 1730, when Tokugawa Muneharu came to power and dissolved the domain. {| class=wikitable |+{{ill|Tachibana clan (samurai)|en|Tachibana clan (samurai)|WD=Q2975377|lt=Tachibana clan}}, 1620–1871 (''{{ill|Tozama daimyō|en|Tozama daimyō|WD=Q1195912|lt=Tozama daimyō}}'') ! #||Name || Tenure || Courtesy title || Court Rank || ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' |- ||1||{{ill|Tachibana Muneshige|en|Tachibana Muneshige|WD=Q1071770|lt=Tachibana Muneshige}} (立花宗茂)||1620–1638|| ''Sakon'e-no-shōgen'' (左近将監) ||Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||109,000 ''koku'' |- ||2||Tachibana Tadashige (立花忠重)||1638–1664|| ''Sakon'e-no-shōgen'' (左近将監) ||Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||109,000 ''koku'' |- ||3||Tachibana Akitora (立花秋虎)||1664–1696||''Sakon'e-no-shōgen'' (左近将監) ||Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||109,000 ''koku'' |- ||4||Tachibana Akitaka (立花明隆)||1696–1721|| ''Hida-no-kami''(飛騨守) ||Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) ||109,000 ''koku'' |- ||5||Tachibana Sadayoshi (立花貞俶)||1721–1744|| ''Hida-no-kami''(飛騨守) ||Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) ||109,000 ''koku'' |- ||6||Tachibana Sadanori (立花貞則)||1744–1746||''Hida-no-kami''(飛騨守) ||Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) ||109,000 ''koku'' |- ||7||Tachibana Akinao (立花鑑通)||1746–1797||''Ukyō-no-daibu''(左京大夫) ||Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) ||109,000 ''koku'' |- ||8||Tachibana Akihisa (立花鑑寿)||1797–1820|| ''Sakon'e-no-shōgen'' (左近将監) ||Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||109,000 ''koku'' |- ||9||Tachibana Akikata (立花鑑賢)||1820–1830|| ''Sakon'e-no-shōgen'' (左近将監) ||Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||109,000 ''koku'' |- ||10||Tachibana Akihiro (立花鑑広)||1830–1833|| -None- ||Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) ||109,000 ''koku'' |- ||11||Tachibana Akinobu (立花鑑備)||1833–1845||''Sakon'e-no-shōgen'' (左近将監) ||Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||109,000 ''koku'' |- ||12||Tachibana Akitomo (立花鑑寛)||1845–1871||''Sakon'e-no-shōgen'' (左近将監) ||Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||109,000 ->114,000 ''koku'' |} ===Takasu Domain=== The {{ill|Takasu Domain|en|Takasu Domain|WD=Q3034539|lt=Takasu Domain}} (高須藩, Takasu-han) was a Japanese domain located in {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino Province}} (present-day {{ill|Kaizu|en|Kaizu|WD=Q844056|lt=Kaizu}}, {{ill|Gifu|en|Gifu|WD=Q45798|lt=Gifu}}). For most of its history, it was ruled by the Takasu-Matsudaira, a branch of the [[Tokugawa clan]] of Owari Domain. Matsudaira Katamori, Matsudaira Sadaaki, Tokugawa Yoshikatsu, and {{ill|Tokugawa Mochinaga|en|Tokugawa Mochinaga|WD=Q946037|lt=Tokugawa Mochinaga}}, four important figures in {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu}}-era Japan, were the sons of Matsudaira Yoshitatsu, one of Takasu's last daimyō. The Takasu Domain also provided 30,000 ''koku'' to the Owari Domain annually from 1700 to 1870, when it was merged with the Owari Domain. {| class=wikitable |+[[File: Japanese crest Owari mitu Aoi.svg|25px]]{{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira clan}}/[[Tokugawa clan]] (''{{ill|Shinpan (daimyo)|en|Shinpan (daimyo)|WD=Q1331112|lt=Shinpan}}'') 1700–1870 ! #||Name || Tenure || Courtesy title || Court Rank || ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' |- ||1||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshiyuki]]| 松平義行}}||1700–1715||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守) || Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||2||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitaka]]| 松平義孝}}||1715–1732||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下)||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||3||{{nihongo|[[:en:Matsudaira Yoshiatsu|Matsudaira Yoshiatsu]]| 松平義淳}}||1732–1739||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左近衛少将) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下)||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||4||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitoshi]]| 松平義敏}}||1739–1771||''Nakatsukasa no-daiyu'' (中務大輔)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||5||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitomo]]|松平義柄}}||1771–1777||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守); ''Jijū'' (侍従) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||6||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaita Yoshihiro]]| 松平 義裕}}||1777–1795||''Settsu-no-kami'' (摂津守); ''Jijū'' (侍従) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||7||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshimasa]]| [松平義当}}||1795–1801||''Danjo-hitsu'' (弾正大弼) || Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||8||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshisue]]| 松平義居}}||1801–1804||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)；''Jijū'' (侍従)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||9||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshinari]]| 松平義和}}||1804–1832|||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||10||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitatsu]]| 松平義建}}||1832–1850||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||11||{{nihongo|[[Tokugawa Chikanaga]]| 徳川茂徳}}||1850–1858||''Sakonoe-shosho'' (左少将)|| Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下) ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||12||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshimasa]]| 松平義端}}||1858–1860|| – none – || – none – ||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||13||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshitake]]| 松平義勇}}||1860–1869|| – none – ||Junior 5th Rank Lower Grade, (従五位)||30,000 ''koku'' |- ||14||{{nihongo|[[Matsudaira Yoshinari]]| 松平義生}}||1869–1870||- none – || – none – ||30,000 ''koku'' |} ==See also== *{{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=Han system}} *[[:en:List of Han|List of Han]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Domains of Chūbu}} [[en:Owari Domain]] [[de:Owari (Han)]] [[fa:قلمرو اواری]] [[fr:Domaine d'Owari]] [[ja:尾張藩]] [[ko:오와리번]] [[ru:Овари (княжество)]] [[th:แคว้นศักดินาโอวาริ]] [[zh:尾張藩]] {{wikidata link|Q1062072}} [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
